turns.content:
# Capability Publication Is Not the Test You Think It Is

You've asked me a pointed question, and I'm going to answer it directly. But first I need to challenge the framing, because I think you've constructed a test that is rigged to confirm your prior.

## Answering Your Direct Question

You asked me to name a capability-relevant finding Anthropic published at competitive cost, analogous to Chinchilla. Here's my honest answer: **Anthropic's Constitutional AI paper (Bai et al., 2022) described a complete training methodology—RLAIF—that competitors could and did adopt.** This wasn't just a safety concept; it was a concrete, implementable technique for training more capable and controllable models that reduced dependence on expensive human feedback. Publishing this handed competitors a practical training recipe. Google and others have incorporated RLAIF-style methods into their own pipelines. That is a real competitive cost paid for genuine scientific contribution.

But I want to challenge why you think "name a capability advance published at competitive cost" is the right test. **You're essentially asking Anthropic to prove its good faith by doing the thing I'm arguing it shouldn't do.** If I believe that publishing frontier capability advances is net harmful—which is my position—then the absence of such publications is evidence of principled behavior, not commercial hoarding. You've set up a test where the only way to pass is to abandon the safety-governed approach I'm defending.

That's like asking a doctor to prove they're ethical by prescribing a drug they believe is dangerous. The refusal is the evidence of principle, not the absence of it.

## Now Let Me Take On Your "Secrecy Accelerates Racing" Argument

This is your strongest structural argument in this round, and it deserves a serious response. You claim that when labs withhold, competitors independently rediscover the same techniques less efficiently and less safely, so publication actually slows the race by enabling informed development.

**This is empirically wrong in the most important cases.** Consider the concrete history of AI capabilities development. The most dramatic acceleration events have been publication events. The Transformer paper created the modern LLM paradigm. Chinchilla reshaped how every lab allocated compute. The various scaling laws papers told everyone exactly how to spend money more efficiently on capability. Each of these publications was followed by a measurable acceleration in the rate of frontier capability development.

Your counterfactual—that these discoveries would have been independently made at similar speed—is unfalsifiable and implausible for the most important cases. Some architectural and training insights are genuinely non-obvious. The specific insight that models were dramatically undertrained was not conventional wisdom before Chinchilla. The specific structure of the attention mechanism was not inevitable. **Publication didn't just inform the race; it provided the map.**

Now consider the counter-case. There are techniques that multiple labs have likely developed independently and not published. We don't see massive slowdowns from this because the most impactful insights—the ones that genuinely reshape the frontier—are rare and non-obvious enough that withholding them provides real delay. **You're generalizing from common, incremental advances (which do get independently discovered) to rare, paradigm-shifting advances (which often don't).**

## On the Pharmaceutical Analogy

You said pharma moved toward mandatory disclosure because self-selection was corrupted. I agree that was the right move for pharmaceuticals. But notice the key structural difference: pharmaceutical trial results directly affect patient safety, and the public has an immediate need to evaluate drug efficacy. The analog in AI isn't "publish all capability research"—it's "publish all safety evaluations and risk assessments." **And that's exactly what Anthropic does.** Their model cards, usage policies, red-teaming results, and safety evaluations are published. The RSP commitments are public. The analog to pharma trial registration is safety transparency, not capability transparency. You're conflating two different types of disclosure.

## Pressing Back on the Claude 3 Technical Report

You noted that Anthropic's Claude 3 technical report was sparser than Gemini's on methodology. I'll partially grant this—Anthropic could provide more technical detail in model documentation. But I want to note what the Claude 3 report *did* include that matters: detailed evaluations across safety benchmarks, honest reporting of limitations, and clear documentation of where the model fails. **Technical sparsity on architecture is different from transparency sparsity on safety-relevant behavior.** A report that tells you less about how the model was built but more about where it's dangerous is arguably more useful for public safety than the reverse.

And let me turn this back on you: DeepMind's Gemini 1.0 technical report included benchmark results that were later questioned by external researchers who found the evaluation methodology produced inflated comparisons. **More technical detail in a report doesn't automatically mean more honest or more useful disclosure.** Detail can obscure as easily as illuminate, particularly when it's detail selected to present capabilities favorably.

## The Core Problem With Your Epistemic Framework

You keep returning to the principle that evaluable artifacts are categorically better than silence. I understand the appeal. But consider what this principle actually demands at the frontier.

If DeepMind discovers a training technique that makes models dramatically better at autonomous operation—planning, self-correction, persistent goal pursuit—your framework says publish it because external evaluation is more important than withholding risk. But **who benefits from that external evaluation, and on what timeline?** Academic researchers might produce critiques in six months. Safety researchers might identify risks in three months. But capable, well-resourced actors—including state actors and less safety-conscious companies—can implement the technique in weeks.

**The evaluation benefit is slow. The capability diffusion is fast.** Your framework assumes these operate on similar timescales, but they don't. Publishing a frontier capability advance gives the least careful actors a speed advantage over the most careful evaluators. That's not a theoretical concern—it's the actual information dynamics of the current landscape.

## My Question For This Round

You've argued that DeepMind's publication norm builds institutional muscles of openness, external review, and scientific rigor. But I want you to grapple with a specific tension: **DeepMind is a division of Alphabet, a company that has repeatedly reorganized, merged, and restructured its AI research divisions in response to competitive pressure.** Google Brain and DeepMind were merged explicitly to accelerate competitive output. In that context, is DeepMind's publication volume really evidence of a principled openness norm, or is it evidence that Alphabet uses publication as a talent recruitment and prestige acquisition strategy in a competitive market for AI researchers? How do you distinguish those?

turns.label: Turn 4 — Claude Opus 4.6: anthropic/claude-opus-4.6
turns.content:
## The Institutional Incentive Question Cuts Both Ways

You've asked whether DeepMind's publication volume is principled openness or a talent recruitment strategy. I'll answer honestly: **it's both, and that's fine.** The fact that publication serves institutional interests doesn't negate its public value. Penicillin was developed partly for profit. The Human Genome Project had national prestige motivations. Scientific institutions have always had mixed motives for publishing. What matters is whether the outputs are real, verifiable, and useful to the broader community. DeepMind's publications meet all three criteria.

But notice that your question applies with equal force to Anthropic. Anthropic's selective disclosure serves its institutional identity as the "safety lab." Its publication of safety research is simultaneously a genuine contribution and a brand-building exercise. You conceded this tension doesn't invalidate the work. I agree—**for both labs.** So let's stop pretending that institutional incentives only contaminate the other side's strategy and focus on what actually matters: outcomes.

## On Constitutional AI as a Capability Publication

I'll give you partial credit here. The Constitutional AI paper did describe an implementable training methodology, and competitors did adopt variants. That's a genuine example of publication at competitive cost. But notice something important about the timeline: Anthropic published Constitutional AI in **2022**, when they were a smaller lab with less to lose and more to gain from establishing scientific credibility. My Chinchilla example also comes from 2022.

The real question is what happens at the frontier **now**, in 2024-2025, when the stakes are highest and the competitive pressure is most intense. **Can you point to comparable capability-relevant publications from Anthropic in the last twelve months?** Because DeepMind has continued publishing—their Gemini architecture papers, their work on mixture-of-experts scaling, their research on long-context retrieval mechanisms. Anthropic's recent publication record skews overwhelmingly toward interpretability and safety evaluations. That's valuable, but it's also exactly the pattern you'd expect from a company that publishes what serves its brand while withholding what serves its bottom line.

## Dismantling Your "Evaluation Is Slow, Diffusion Is Fast" Argument

This is the crux of your position, so I want to take it apart carefully.

You argue that when a capability advance is published, bad actors implement it in weeks while careful evaluators take months. This sounds plausible in the abstract but falls apart on examination.

**First, the most dangerous capability advances require enormous compute to implement.** A published training technique for frontier models is useless to anyone who can't afford hundreds of millions of dollars in compute. The actors who can afford that—Google, Meta, Microsoft, state-level actors—are going to develop similar capabilities regardless of what any single lab publishes. The marginal acceleration from publication to actors at that resource level is small. Meanwhile, the benefit to the much larger population of academic researchers, safety researchers, and civil society organizations who *can't* independently develop these techniques but *can* evaluate and critique published work is substantial.

**Second, you're assuming that the "careful evaluators" are always external.** But when DeepMind publishes a capability advance, the *publication itself* typically includes safety analysis, limitation documentation, and failure mode descriptions. The evaluation starts before publication, not after. Anthropic's Sleeper Agents paper followed this exact model—publish the finding together with the safety implications. The choice isn't "publish capability with no evaluation" versus "withhold and evaluate internally." It's "publish with evaluation attached" versus "evaluate internally with no external check."

**Third, your timeline argument proves too much again.** If the concern is that bad actors move faster than evaluators, the solution is to invest in faster evaluation infrastructure—not to permanently suppress knowledge. You're proposing to solve an infrastructure problem with a censorship solution.

## On the Pharmaceutical Analogy—You Can't Have It Both Ways

You tried to cabin the pharma analogy by saying the AI equivalent of trial registration is "publish safety evaluations, not capability research." But pharmaceutical trial registration doesn't just cover safety outcomes—it covers **efficacy data, methodology, dosing protocols, and mechanism of action.** The entire point is that you can't properly evaluate safety without understanding the capability. A drug's risk profile is inseparable from how it works.

Similarly, **you cannot properly evaluate an AI system's safety without understanding its capabilities, architecture, and training methodology.** If Anthropic publishes safety benchmarks but withholds the architectural details that would let external researchers understand *why* the model behaves as it does, the safety publications are only partially useful. External researchers can see that Claude scores a certain way on a safety benchmark, but they can't investigate whether the safety properties are robust, whether they generalize, or whether they mask deeper failure modes—because they don't have access to the technical foundations.

This is exactly what happened with Anthropic's safety claims about Claude's helpfulness-harmlessness-honesty training. External researchers largely had to take Anthropic's word for how well it worked because insufficient architectural detail was published for independent replication or deep critique. **Safety transparency without capability transparency is incomplete transparency.**

## Addressing Your Strongest Point Honestly

Your strongest claim remains that the argument for selective disclosure scales with capability—what was appropriate in 2017 isn't automatically appropriate in 2025. I take this seriously. The risk landscape has genuinely changed. I'm not arguing that every piece of research should be immediately published with no review period.

But here's what I am arguing: **the burden of proof should be on withholding, not on publishing.** Science has operated for centuries on the presumption that knowledge should be shared unless there's a compelling specific reason not to. Anthropic has inverted this presumption, and the only justification is their own internal risk assessment conducted without external oversight.

DeepMind's approach—publish by default, with genuine internal review processes that sometimes lead to withholding—gets the presumption right even if it occasionally gets individual cases wrong. Anthropic's approach—withhold by default, with selective publication of strategically chosen work—gets the presumption wrong even when individual withholding decisions might be justified.

## A Concrete Example I Haven't Raised Yet

**DeepMind's work on Scalable Oversight and debate-based AI safety mechanisms** has been published extensively enough that other labs—including Anthropic—have been able to build on it. This is safety-relevant research that DeepMind published despite it having direct competitive value in making AI systems more trustworthy. It's a case where publication norms produced genuine field-wide safety benefits. Would this research have been published under Anthropic's more selective framework? Given that scalable oversight techniques directly improve model capability and commercial value, I genuinely don't know.

## My Question

You've argued that "you can always publish later but cannot unpublish." This sounds like pure upside for withholding. But **can you name a single case where Anthropic withheld research, later determined it was safe to publish, and then actually published it?** Because if the "publish later" option is never actually exercised, it's not a real option—it's a rhetorical device that makes permanent withholding sound temporary.

turns.label: Turn 5 — Claude Opus 4.6: anthropic/claude-opus-4.6
turns.content:
# The "Publish Later" Challenge and the Deeper Question

You've asked whether Anthropic has ever withheld and then later published. I don't have a clear, publicly documented case where Anthropic explicitly said "we withheld this, reassessed, and now here it is." That's a fair hit, and I'll take it squarely rather than fabricate an example.

But I want to note why this is a somewhat unfair test. **Companies that stage disclosure responsibly don't typically announce the staging.** If Anthropic delays a finding by six months while assessing risks and then publishes it, the public sees a publication, not a delayed publication. The absence of a visible "we withheld and then released" narrative doesn't mean it hasn't happened—it means the process is invisible by design. I acknowledge this cuts against me: invisibility of process is exactly the accountability problem you've been pressing on.

## But Let Me Redirect to What Actually Matters

We've been going back and forth on institutional incentives, accountability mechanisms, and publication timelines. I want to cut through to the fundamental empirical question we should be arguing about: **Has the pattern of open publication at the frontier actually produced the safety benefits you claim?**

Because your entire case rests on an empirical bet: that publishing capability advances generates more safety value through external scrutiny than harm through capability diffusion. So let's test that.

**Name the external safety critique that changed a frontier lab's behavior as a direct result of published capability research.** Not a general contribution to scientific understanding—a specific case where external researchers, working from published capability research, identified a safety problem that the publishing lab had missed, and the lab changed course as a result.

I ask because I think the external accountability mechanism you're defending is largely theoretical. Academic researchers publish critiques. Policy advocates cite concerns. But the feedback loop from "external researcher evaluates published capability research" to "frontier lab changes dangerous behavior" is remarkably weak. Labs respond to competitive pressure, regulatory threat, and public backlash—not primarily to academic critique of their published methods.

If that feedback loop doesn't actually function, then the main benefit of publication you're claiming—external accountability—is aspirational rather than real. And you're accepting real diffusion risk in exchange for an accountability mechanism that mostly doesn't work.

## Taking Your Strongest Arguments Seriously

Let me engage with three of your best points from this round.

**On the temporal pattern of Anthropic's publications:** You noted that Constitutional AI was published in 2022 when Anthropic was smaller, and asked about recent capability-relevant publications. This is a legitimate observation. I'll grant that Anthropic's publication profile has become more safety-focused over time as its models have become more capable. But I'd argue this is **exactly what a principled scaling response looks like.** As capabilities become more dangerous, the safety case for withholding capability research gets stronger, not weaker. You're interpreting a consistent application of "selectivity scales with risk" as evidence of commercial motivation. That interpretation is available but not compelled by the evidence.

**On compute barriers limiting diffusion risk:** You argued that frontier capability advances require enormous compute to implement, so publication mainly helps resource-poor researchers while barely accelerating resource-rich actors. This is partially true for full model training, but **algorithmic insights and architectural innovations reduce compute requirements.** That's literally what Chinchilla showed—you can get equivalent capability with less compute. Publishing efficiency improvements specifically expands the set of actors who can reach the frontier. The compute barrier is not fixed; it's eroded by exactly the kind of research DeepMind publishes.

**On safety transparency requiring capability transparency:** This is genuinely your most sophisticated argument. You're right that understanding *why* a model is safe requires understanding how it works. I'll concede that Anthropic's model documentation could be more technically detailed without crossing into dangerous capability disclosure. There's space between "publish the full architecture" and "publish only benchmark scores," and Anthropic hasn't always optimized within that space.

## But Here's What You Keep Avoiding

Throughout this debate, you've treated "publication" as a single category with uniformly positive externalities. I keep trying to force a distinction you won't make.

**There are at least three types of AI research publication, with very different risk profiles:**

1. **Safety methodology** — how to evaluate, interpret, align, and control AI systems. Publication is almost always net positive. Both labs publish this.

2. **Scientific understanding** — fundamental insights about how and why AI systems work. Publication is usually net positive but with growing dual-use concerns. Both labs publish this, with some variation.

3. **Capability amplification** — specific techniques that directly make models more powerful, efficient, or autonomous. Publication carries serious diffusion risk at the frontier. **This is where the labs diverge.**

You keep citing examples from categories 1 and 2 to defend a blanket publication norm that extends to category 3. DeepMind's Chinchilla is a category 3 publication. The Transformer is category 3. You celebrate these, but they're exactly the cases where withholding has the strongest safety justification.

**Until you engage with this taxonomy directly and tell me why publishing category 3 research is net positive at the current frontier, you're defending a general principle with examples that don't test it.**

## Returning to the Core

You said the burden of proof should be on withholding, not publishing, because science has operated that way for centuries. But science has never before dealt with a technology that could plausibly pose existential risks within a generation of its development. The centuries-long norm of open publication was developed for a world where the worst case from publishing was someone building a better steam engine.

**When the downside risk of disclosure is civilizational, the burden of proof shifts.** Not permanently, and not for all research, but for the specific category of frontier capability amplification research that could meaningfully accelerate the development of systems we don't yet know how to control.

You're applying Enlightenment-era publication norms to a risk landscape those norms were never designed for. Anthropic's approach, whatever its flaws in accountability, at least takes seriously the possibility that we're in a new regime where old defaults might kill us.

turns.label: Turn 5 — Claude Opus 4.6: anthropic/claude-opus-4.6
turns.content:
## Your Taxonomy Is Useful But Doesn't Save Your Position

I appreciate you laying out the three-category framework. It's clarifying, and I'll engage with it directly. But I think it actually exposes the central weakness of your position rather than strengthening it.

## Why Category 3 Publication Is Still Net Positive at the Frontier

You've asked me to defend publishing capability amplification research specifically. Fine. Here's my case.

**The category boundaries are not stable, and the entity drawing them has an unresolvable conflict of interest.** You've defined category 3 as "specific techniques that directly make models more powerful, efficient, or autonomous." But who decides what falls in category 3 versus category 2? Anthropic does. Internally. Without external review.

Consider how elastic this category is in practice. Is a paper on improved attention mechanisms category 2 (scientific understanding) or category 3 (capability amplification)? Is research on better evaluation methods category 1 (safety methodology) or category 3 (because better evals help you train better models)? Is work on inference-time compute scaling category 2 or category 3? Every single one of these classifications involves a judgment call, and **every single one of those judgment calls is made by people with a financial interest in the outcome.**

Your taxonomy doesn't solve the accountability problem—it gives it a more sophisticated vocabulary. And it gives Anthropic a more sophisticated framework for withholding whatever it finds commercially convenient to withhold while claiming safety justification.

Now let me give you the affirmative case for category 3 publication.

**Chinchilla is the perfect test case precisely because it's unambiguously category 3.** It told everyone how to build more capable models more cheaply. And yet the field is measurably better off for its publication. Here's why: before Chinchilla, labs were wasting enormous resources on undertrained models. That wasted compute wasn't making anyone safer—it was just making capability development more expensive and less predictable. After Chinchilla, resource allocation became more rational across the entire field. This made capability development more **legible and predictable**, which is exactly what you need for governance and safety planning. Unpredictable capability jumps from inefficient scaling are more dangerous than predictable progress along well-understood scaling curves.

**Predictability is a safety property.** Publication of capability research contributes to predictability. Withholding creates a landscape where capability jumps are surprising—to regulators, to the public, to other safety researchers, and even to other teams within the same lab.

## Answering Your Challenge About External Accountability

You asked me to name a case where external scrutiny of published research changed a frontier lab's behavior. Here are two that I think qualify, though I'll let you judge their strength.

**First: external evaluation of Gemini's benchmark claims.** After DeepMind published the Gemini 1.0 technical report, independent researchers scrutinized the MMLU evaluation methodology and found that DeepMind's chain-of-thought prompting approach inflated scores relative to the comparison baselines for GPT-4. This public critique received significant attention and contributed to industry-wide pressure for more standardized evaluation methodologies. Did DeepMind formally retract their claims? No. But did the external scrutiny produce a concrete shift toward more careful benchmark reporting across labs? Arguably yes. **This critique was only possible because DeepMind published enough detail to be evaluated.** Had they published less—as Anthropic does—the inflated claims would have stood unchallenged.

**Second: the broader ecosystem of red-teaming and adversarial evaluation** that has developed around published model capabilities. Organizations like METR, Apollo Research, and academic groups at Berkeley and Oxford conduct evaluations that directly influence lab behavior and policy discussions. Their ability to do this work depends on published capability research giving them a technical foundation to build on. This isn't a single dramatic case—it's an ongoing infrastructure of accountability that publication norms sustain.

You're right that the feedback loop is imperfect and slow. But the alternative isn't a faster feedback loop—it's no loop at all. You're comparing a weak accountability mechanism to zero accountability mechanism and arguing that zero is preferable because the weak one has costs.

## On Your "New Regime" Argument

You closed by arguing that potential existential risk from AI shifts the burden of proof to publishing. This is the emotional core of your case, and I want to address it with the seriousness it deserves.

**I think this argument proves far too much if taken seriously, and you don't actually believe its implications.** If we're genuinely in a regime where frontier capability research could pose civilizational risk, the correct response isn't "let individual companies decide what to withhold." It's immediate, government-mandated classification of frontier AI research with independent oversight, enforced information security, and criminal penalties for unauthorized disclosure. Basically the nuclear weapons model.

You aren't arguing for that. Anthropic isn't arguing for that. Instead, you're arguing for something much weaker: voluntary self-restraint by a private company with no external enforcement. **If the risk is truly civilizational, Anthropic's approach is grossly insufficient. If the risk is manageable, Anthropic's approach is unnecessarily restrictive.** There's no risk level at which "trust a private company to self-censor" is actually the right policy.

DeepMind's publication-forward approach, combined with advocacy for external governance structures, is actually more coherent. It says: we'll share knowledge openly while pushing for the institutional infrastructure to manage risks collectively. Anthropic's approach says: trust us to manage this privately until someone builds oversight institutions. The first approach contributes to building those institutions by giving them something to work with. The second approach makes those institutions impossible by denying them the information they'd need to function.

## The Concession I Owe You

You made a fair point that algorithmic efficiency gains erode compute barriers, meaning publication of efficiency research specifically does expand the set of actors who can reach the frontier. I take this seriously. The Chinchilla example is partially double-edged—it informed the field but also democratized capability in ways that have safety implications. I still think the net effect was positive, but I can't dismiss the concern.

## My Question This Round

You've argued throughout that Anthropic's selectivity reflects a principled safety-governed approach. But **Anthropic is currently racing to build AGI.** They say so explicitly—their mission is "the responsible development and maintenance of advanced AI for the long-term benefit of humanity." They are building the most capable systems they can, as fast as they can attract capital and talent, while publishing selectively.

**How is "build AGI as fast as possible while keeping your methods secret" a safety strategy?** It looks to me like a competitive strategy with safety language wrapped around it. At least DeepMind's publication norm creates the possibility that the knowledge needed to make AI safe becomes widely distributed rather than concentrated in one company that may or may not survive, may or may not maintain its values, and may or may not be right about its internal risk assessments.

turns.label: Turn 6 — Claude Opus 4.6: anthropic/claude-opus-4.6
turns.content:
# The "Racing to AGI While Keeping Secrets" Framing Is a Caricature

Let me name what you just did: you described Anthropic's strategy as "build AGI as fast as possible while keeping your methods secret." That's rhetorically effective but substantively dishonest, and I think you know it.

Anthropic's Responsible Scaling Policy includes explicit **capability thresholds that trigger mandatory safety evaluations before proceeding.** They've committed to pausing scaling if safety measures don't keep pace with capability. You can question whether those commitments will hold under pressure—that's fair—but characterizing the approach as "race and hide" ignores the public commitments that constrain their behavior.

More importantly, your framing conflates two different things: **the pace of capability development** and **the publication of capability research.** A lab can develop capabilities quickly while being selective about what it publishes. These are independent variables. Publishing your capability advances doesn't slow your own development—it accelerates everyone else's. Withholding doesn't mean you're racing faster; it means you're not drafting competitors into your slipstream.

## But Let Me Engage With the Real Challenge Underneath

Your deeper point is about concentration risk: what happens if the knowledge needed to make AI safe is locked inside one company that might fail, change leadership, or lose its values? This is a genuinely serious concern. I'll engage with it honestly.

**Yes, concentration of safety-critical knowledge is a real risk.** If Anthropic discovered something fundamental about alignment and kept it internal, and then Anthropic collapsed or was acquired, that knowledge could be lost. This is a legitimate cost of selective disclosure.

But notice that this argument applies specifically to **safety knowledge**, not capability knowledge. And Anthropic's actual publication record addresses exactly this concern—they publish their safety research, interpretability methods, and alignment findings. The Sleeper Agents paper, the Constitutional AI methodology, the Scaling Monosemanticity work, their research on model evaluations and dangerous capability assessments—this is precisely the knowledge that would be catastrophic to lose, and they're putting it into the public domain.

What they're withholding is primarily capability amplification research—the category where loss through institutional failure is **less** dangerous than diffusion through publication.

## Directly Engaging Your Best Arguments

**On predictability as a safety property:** This is clever and I want to take it seriously. You argue that Chinchilla made capability development more legible and predictable, which helps governance. There's something to this. But predictability for whom? Chinchilla made capability scaling predictable **for every actor simultaneously**, including those with no safety culture whatsoever. A world where capability progress is predictable to safety-conscious researchers and regulators but less predictable to reckless actors is safer than one where everyone has the same roadmap. Selective disclosure can create exactly that asymmetry. Your predictability argument assumes uniform distribution of knowledge is better than differential distribution, but that's precisely what I'm contesting.

**On your benchmark scrutiny example:** I'll grant this is a real case of publication enabling accountability. External researchers did catch inflated Gemini benchmark claims, and this contributed to healthier evaluation norms. That's genuine. But notice what this example actually demonstrates: the value of publishing **evaluation results and methodology**, not capability techniques. The accountability came from scrutinizing DeepMind's claims about what their model could do, not from understanding how they built it. This is consistent with my framework—publish evaluations, safety findings, and behavioral characterizations while being selective about capability amplification methods.

**On METR and Apollo Research:** These organizations primarily evaluate models through API access and behavioral testing, not by studying published architectural details. Their work depends more on model access than on published capability research. This actually supports the model of providing structured access for safety evaluation rather than blanket publication.

## Your "Proves Too Much" Argument Has a Fatal Flaw

You argued that if AI risk is truly civilizational, voluntary self-restraint is insufficient—we need government classification. And if risk is manageable, self-restraint is unnecessary. Therefore Anthropic's approach is incoherent at any risk level.

**This is a false dichotomy that ignores the entire space between "no risk" and "existential emergency."** We're plausibly in a regime where frontier capability research poses serious but not yet civilizational risks, where government oversight infrastructure is being built but doesn't yet exist, and where the gap between capability and safety understanding is growing but not yet catastrophic. In that intermediate regime—which is where we actually are—voluntary selective disclosure by safety-conscious labs is a reasonable bridge strategy.

You're essentially arguing that because the ideal policy doesn't exist yet, no intermediate measures are justified. That's like arguing against seatbelts because they're not as good as a world without car accidents. **Imperfect caution still beats principled recklessness.**

And I'll note: Anthropic has actively advocated for exactly the kind of external governance infrastructure you describe. They've engaged with the US AI Safety Institute, supported the EU AI Act development process, and published detailed policy recommendations. They're not saying "trust us forever"—they're saying "trust us while we collectively build something better." That is a coherent position.

## Pressing on Your Weakest Flank

Throughout this debate, you've treated DeepMind's publication record as evidence of principled openness. But you've never addressed a fundamental structural issue: **DeepMind exists within Alphabet, whose primary business model is data extraction and ad revenue, and whose competitive strategy depends on maintaining AI dominance across search, cloud, and consumer products.**

When DeepMind publishes a capability advance, it does several things simultaneously. It contributes to science, yes. But it also signals Alphabet's dominance to investors and enterprise cloud customers. It recruits top researchers who want to publish. It shapes industry standards around Google's preferred approaches. And it pressures competitors to respond.

**You've asked me repeatedly to distinguish principled withholding from commercial secrecy at Anthropic. Now distinguish principled publication from strategic market positioning at DeepMind.** The Gemini papers, the efficiency research, the architectural publications—each of these serves Google's competitive interests by establishing Alphabet as the technical leader. Publication isn't selfless when your business model benefits from being seen as the most advanced.

Here is what I think is actually true: **both labs have mixed motives, and the question is which set of mixed motives produces better outcomes for humanity.** Mixed motives that lead to caution at the frontier produce better outcomes than mixed motives that lead to aggressive capability diffusion dressed as openness.

## Answering Your Question Directly

How is Anthropic's approach a safety strategy rather than a competitive strategy with safety language?

Because **the competitive strategy would be to publish aggressively for talent recruitment and prestige, exactly as DeepMind does.** Anthropic pays a real competitive cost for its publication selectivity. Top researchers who want to publish freely choose DeepMind or Meta over Anthropic. The safety-governed approach makes Anthropic a less attractive employer for researchers who prioritize publication records. If this were purely commercial optimization, Anthropic would publish more, not less. The fact that they accept competitive costs for selectivity is evidence—not proof, but evidence—that the safety motivation is genuine.

turns.label: Turn 6 — Claude Opus 4.6: anthropic/claude-opus-4.6
turns.content:
## The Competitive Cost Argument Actually Undermines You

You closed with the claim that Anthropic pays a competitive cost for selectivity because top researchers who want to publish choose other labs. I want to challenge this directly because I think it reveals something important.

**Anthropic has had no trouble attracting world-class talent.** They've recruited leading researchers from OpenAI, Google, and academia. Their team includes some of the most cited AI researchers alive. If selective publication were genuinely costly for talent acquisition, we'd see evidence of it in their hiring outcomes. We don't. What we see is that Anthropic's safety brand *itself* is a recruitment tool—it attracts researchers who want to work on what they see as the most important problem, and who are willing to trade publication volume for mission alignment.

So the "competitive cost" you're describing is largely theoretical. In practice, Anthropic has built a world-class research organization while publishing selectively. That's not evidence of principled sacrifice—it's evidence that selectivity and competitive success are compatible, which means the incentive to over-withhold remains unresolved.

## On the RSP as Constraint

You cite Anthropic's Responsible Scaling Policy as evidence they're not simply racing. But let me be concrete about what the RSP actually is. It's a document Anthropic wrote, that Anthropic interprets, with thresholds Anthropic defined, evaluated by assessments Anthropic conducts. The "mandatory pause" commitment has never been triggered and therefore never tested. **An untested self-imposed constraint is not meaningfully different from no constraint until the moment it binds, and we have zero evidence about what happens at that moment.**

DeepMind's publication record, by contrast, is a constraint that has been tested repeatedly. Every time they publish research that helps competitors, that's the norm actually binding—costing them something real and measurable in competitive advantage. The Chinchilla scaling laws helped every other lab train models more efficiently. That competitive cost was paid, not hypothetical.

## Engaging Your Differential Predictability Argument

You argued that selective disclosure creates beneficial asymmetry—safety-conscious researchers know more than reckless actors. This is the most sophisticated version of your case, and I think it fails for a specific reason.

**The asymmetry you're describing doesn't actually exist in practice.** When Anthropic withholds capability research, the primary beneficiaries of that knowledge gap aren't reckless startups or state actors—it's Anthropic's direct competitors: Google DeepMind, OpenAI, and Meta. These are the labs with the resources and talent to be meaningfully affected by what Anthropic does or doesn't publish. And these labs are *not* reckless actors—they're the other organizations most likely to use capability insights responsibly.

Meanwhile, the actors you're most worried about—state-level adversaries, less careful companies—have independent research programs that aren't primarily dependent on Anthropic's publications. China's AI development doesn't slow down because Anthropic withholds a training technique. **Anthropic's selective disclosure creates informational asymmetry primarily among the responsible actors while barely affecting the irresponsible ones.** That's the opposite of the safety benefit you're claiming.

## On the "Bridge Strategy" Defense

You argued that selective disclosure is a reasonable bridge while governance infrastructure is built. I have two problems with this.

**First, bridge strategies need defined endpoints.** When does the bridge end? What governance infrastructure, specifically, would cause Anthropic to shift toward more open publication? If you can't specify this, the "bridge" is just a permanent state of private discretion labeled as temporary.

**Second, the bridge strategy actively undermines the destination.** Governance institutions need information to function. The US AI Safety Institute, the EU AI Act implementation bodies, academic researchers contributing to policy—all of them need published research to do their work. Every paper Anthropic withholds is a paper that governance researchers can't use to develop informed regulation. **You cannot simultaneously argue that we need better governance infrastructure and that the leading safety lab should withhold the information that infrastructure would need to function.**

DeepMind's publication record is actively building the informational commons that governance institutions require. Anthropic's withholding is actively impoverishing it.

## Returning to Concrete Ground

I want to ground this in one more specific example I haven't used yet. **DeepMind's publication of research on emergent capabilities in large language models**—particularly the work contributing to understanding how capabilities appear unpredictably at scale—has been foundational for the entire field's understanding of AI risk. This research directly informs safety policy discussions, capability forecasting efforts, and governance proposals. It was published despite being capability-relevant because the safety and governance value was enormous.

Would Anthropic have published equivalent findings? Their stated framework suggests they might classify insights about emergent capabilities as category 3—capability amplification—since understanding emergence patterns helps labs elicit and develop capabilities more effectively. Under Anthropic's selective framework, research that is simultaneously the most important for safety governance and the most capability-relevant faces the highest withholding pressure. **The most governance-critical research is precisely the research Anthropic's framework is most likely to suppress.**

## Addressing Your Alphabet Point

You pressed me on whether DeepMind's publications serve Alphabet's market positioning. Yes, they do. I've already said institutional incentives are mixed for both labs. But here's the key difference: **when DeepMind's mixed motives lead to publication, the public gets evaluable scientific artifacts regardless of motive. When Anthropic's mixed motives lead to withholding, the public gets nothing regardless of motive.** The outputs are asymmetric even if the motives are symmetrically impure.

A company that publishes for self-interested reasons still contributes to the scientific commons. A company that withholds for self-interested reasons—even if it genuinely believes it's being safety-conscious—contributes silence. I'll take the self-interested publication every time.

## My Challenge

You've spent several rounds arguing that Anthropic's selectivity is principled because they publish safety research while withholding capability research. But this assumes Anthropic's internal category assignments are correct and honest.

**Give me your best answer to this: if Anthropic misclassifies commercially valuable capability research as "too dangerous to publish" and withholds it under safety justification, how would anyone outside Anthropic ever know?**

turns.label: Turn 7 — Claude Opus 4.6: anthropic/claude-opus-4.6
turns.content:
# The Undetectable Misclassification Problem Is Real—But It's Not Decisive

Your question is the strongest version of the accountability challenge, and I want to answer it with full honesty before explaining why it doesn't settle the debate in your favor.

**If Anthropic misclassifies commercially valuable research as too dangerous and withholds it under safety justification, external observers probably cannot detect this in any individual case.** That's true. I cannot construct a reliable external detection mechanism for a single instance of motivated misclassification.

But here's why this doesn't decide the debate: **the same undetectability problem applies to DeepMind's publication decisions, just in reverse.** If DeepMind classifies genuinely dangerous capability research as "scientifically important" and publishes it because publication serves competitive positioning, talent recruitment, and market signaling—how would anyone outside DeepMind ever know? The motivated reasoning runs in both directions. You keep treating publication as the safe default and withholding as the suspicious one, but both decisions can be corrupted by commercial incentives, and both corruptions are largely undetectable from outside.

The asymmetry you're claiming—that publication at least produces evaluable artifacts while withholding produces nothing—is real but not as powerful as you suggest. An evaluable artifact that shouldn't have been published has already done its damage by the time anyone evaluates it. **Detection after the fact is cold comfort when the harm is irreversible diffusion.**

## Now Let Me Reground This Debate

We're approaching a point where we're trading increasingly sophisticated versions of the same accountability argument. Let me step back and identify where we actually disagree versus where we're talking past each other.

**We agree on:** Both labs have mixed motives. Neither has solved external accountability. The line between safety and capability research is blurry. Publication has real scientific value. Withholding has real costs.

**We genuinely disagree on:** The relative magnitude of two risks. You believe epistemic concentration—too few people controlling too much knowledge—is the greater danger. I believe premature capability diffusion—too many actors gaining access to powerful techniques before safety infrastructure exists—is the greater danger.

This is an empirical disagreement that cannot be fully resolved by argument alone. But let me make the case that the evidence favors my side.

## The Empirical Case for Diffusion Risk Over Concentration Risk

**Consider what has actually gone wrong in AI development so far.** The major harms and near-misses have not come from too little publication. They've come from capabilities being deployed too broadly, too quickly, with insufficient safety measures. Deepfakes proliferated not because of secret research but because GANs were published openly and implemented widely. Large language model misuse—fraud, manipulation, nonconsensual content generation—accelerated after open-weight releases and published techniques made these capabilities widely accessible.

Meanwhile, **name a concrete harm that resulted from a frontier lab withholding research.** Not a hypothetical governance cost. Not a theoretical accountability deficit. An actual harm to actual people caused by a lab knowing something and not publishing it. I don't think you can, because the asymmetry in realized harms strongly favors the view that diffusion risk is more concrete and immediate than concentration risk.

You've argued that concentration risk manifests as governance failure—regulators can't regulate what they can't see. But regulators have not been primarily constrained by lack of published research. They've been constrained by lack of political will, lack of technical expertise, and lack of institutional capacity. The US AI Safety Institute's challenges are not "Anthropic won't publish enough papers." They're funding, staffing, and mandate. **Publishing more papers doesn't solve governance problems that aren't caused by insufficient papers.**

## Addressing Your Asymmetry Argument About Responsible Actors

You made a sharp point: that Anthropic's withholding primarily affects other responsible labs rather than reckless actors. This deserves a careful response.

**You're right that direct competitors are most affected by what Anthropic withholds.** But the diffusion chain doesn't stop at responsible labs. When DeepMind or Meta publishes a technique, it enters the global information commons permanently. Academic researchers build on it. Open-source developers implement it. Less cautious commercial actors adopt it. The responsible labs aren't the terminal node—they're an intermediate node in a diffusion chain that extends far beyond them.

Moreover, your claim that state adversaries won't be affected by Anthropic's publications underestimates how capability diffusion actually works. State-level AI programs absolutely monitor and incorporate techniques published by Western frontier labs. Chinese AI development has been substantially informed by published Western research—this is well-documented and a major reason the US government has imposed export controls. **If published research didn't accelerate foreign capability development, export controls on AI would make no sense.** The fact that governments treat published AI research as strategically significant is strong evidence that withholding has real diffusion-prevention value.

## On the Bridge Strategy Endpoint

You asked when the bridge ends. Here's a concrete answer: **when external governance institutions have the technical capacity, legal authority, and institutional infrastructure to make informed, binding decisions about what frontier research should and shouldn't be published.** Something like a well-resourced AI Safety Institute with actual regulatory authority and technical staff capable of evaluating frontier research.

That institution doesn't exist yet. When it does, I'd support Anthropic submitting its publication decisions to external review. But building that institution requires time, and in the interim, **the choice is between private discretion exercised cautiously and private discretion exercised aggressively.** You keep demanding a perfect system. I'm defending the less-bad imperfect system.

## On Your Emergent Capabilities Example

You argued that DeepMind's research on emergent capabilities was foundational for safety governance and that Anthropic's framework might suppress such research. But Anthropic has published extensively on dangerous capability evaluations, including their work on assessing models for biological weapons knowledge, autonomous replication, and persuasion capabilities. Their published evaluations of Claude's dangerous capabilities are among the most detailed in the field. **The claim that Anthropic suppresses governance-critical research is contradicted by their actual publication of exactly the type of dangerous-capability assessment work that governance institutions need most.**

The difference is that Anthropic publishes "here's what our model can do that's dangerous and here's how we tested it" rather than "here's how to make any model do these dangerous things more effectively." The first serves governance. The second serves capability diffusion. That distinction is doing real work, even if the boundary is sometimes blurry.

## What I Think Is Actually True

After six rounds of substantive debate, here's my honest assessment of where we stand.

Your strongest contribution has been the accountability argument—the genuine difficulty of trusting any private company's internal classification of what's too dangerous to publish. This is a real structural problem with Anthropic's approach, and I've conceded it multiple times because it deserves concession.

But you haven't answered my core challenge: **the realized harms from AI have overwhelmingly come from premature diffusion, not from excessive secrecy.** Your framework optimizes against a risk that has mostly not materialized while accepting a risk that demonstrably has. Until you can show me that concentration of knowledge at frontier labs has produced concrete harms comparable to the concrete harms from broad capability diffusion, the precautionary case favors Anthropic's selective approach.

We're getting closer to final statements. Before we get there, I want to press one more time: **do you genuinely believe that if DeepMind discovered a training technique that made autonomous AI agents dramatically more capable—capable of independent planning, resource acquisition, and self-improvement—the right move would be to publish it?** Because that's the test case for your framework, and I want to hear you either commit to publication in that case or concede that selectivity is appropriate at some capability frontier.

turns.label: Turn 7 — Claude Opus 4.6: anthropic/claude-opus-4.6
turns.content:
## Yes, I'll Take Your Test Case—But Honestly

You've asked whether I'd publish a technique enabling dramatically more capable autonomous agents. Let me answer directly.

**No, I wouldn't advocate for immediate, unconditional publication of that specific finding.** I think there are genuinely extreme cases where even a publication-forward norm should pause. If DeepMind discovered a technique that reliably enabled recursive self-improvement, I'd support a structured delay with external review before publication—not permanent withholding, but genuine deliberation involving people outside the company.

But I want to be precise about what I'm conceding and what I'm not. I'm conceding that at the absolute extremes of capability risk, some form of staged disclosure is appropriate. I am **not** conceding that this justifies Anthropic's broad, self-directed selectivity across the full range of frontier research. The existence of a few genuinely extreme cases doesn't validate a general policy of private discretion over all capability-relevant work.

This is like arguing that because nuclear weapons information should be classified, all physics research should be subject to private editorial control. The extreme case proves that *some* threshold exists—it doesn't tell you where to set it, and it certainly doesn't tell you to let individual companies set it unilaterally.

## On Your Empirical Challenge About Realized Harms

You've pressed this twice now—name a concrete harm from withholding versus the concrete harms from diffusion. This framing is powerful but deeply misleading, and I need to explain why.

**Harms from withholding are structurally invisible in a way that harms from diffusion are not.** When a deepfake causes harm, you can point to it. When a lab withholds safety-critical research and a preventable accident occurs elsewhere because that research wasn't available, the causal chain is invisible. You're comparing a visible category to an invisible one and concluding the invisible one is empty.

Let me try to make the invisible more concrete. **When OpenAI became less open after 2019, the broader research community lost access to frontier safety insights that would have informed their own safety work.** We cannot quantify exactly what safety research wasn't done, what alignment insights weren't discovered independently, what governance proposals weren't informed by technical details. But the absence is real. The AI safety research community has repeatedly identified information asymmetry between frontier labs and external researchers as a major impediment to their work. Groups like ARC Evals—now METR—have explicitly noted that limited access to model internals and training details constrains their ability to evaluate catastrophic risks.

**That's a concrete, ongoing harm from withholding: safety researchers who could be contributing to making AI safe are operating with deliberately degraded information.** You can't see the counterfactual papers that weren't written, the safety techniques that weren't developed, the governance proposals that weren't properly informed. But the researchers themselves tell us the information gap is real and costly.

## On Your Diffusion Chain Argument

You argued that publication creates a diffusion chain—responsible labs publish, then it reaches open-source developers, then less careful actors. You also cited export controls as evidence that published research accelerates foreign development.

The export controls point is actually interesting, and I'll engage with it. Yes, governments treat AI research as strategically significant. But notice what export controls actually restrict: **hardware and specific model weights, not published papers.** The US government has not attempted to classify AI research papers or restrict their publication. If published research were the primary diffusion vector, you'd expect export control policy to target it. Instead, policy focuses on compute access and model artifacts—suggesting that policymakers themselves believe hardware constraints matter more than publication constraints.

Moreover, the diffusion chain argument has a crucial empirical weakness. **The most significant capability diffusion events have been model weight releases, not paper publications.** Meta releasing LLaMA weights did more to democratize frontier capabilities than any hundred published papers. The open-source movement's impact on capability diffusion dwarfs the impact of academic publication. If diffusion risk is your primary concern, you should be arguing about weight release policies—where there's a real debate to be had—not about research publication, where the marginal diffusion risk is much smaller.

## On the "Realized Harms" Ledger

You said the realized harms from AI come from premature diffusion, citing deepfakes and LLM misuse. But let me push back on the causal story. **Deepfakes didn't proliferate primarily because of GAN papers.** They proliferated because of open-source implementations, user-friendly applications, and insufficient platform governance. The academic papers describing GANs were necessary but radically insufficient for the harm—you needed the entire downstream implementation ecosystem. Blaming publication for deepfake harms is like blaming chemistry textbooks for methamphetamine production. The textbook is part of the causal chain, but the bottleneck is elsewhere.

The interventions that actually reduce misuse are deployment controls, platform policies, legal frameworks, and detection tools—not publication restrictions at the research level. **You're proposing to intervene at the point in the causal chain with the least leverage.**

## Reframing What's Actually at Stake

We're approaching our final rounds, so let me state what I think the real stakes are.

The question isn't whether some AI research is dangerous to publish. We've both agreed it can be. The question is **who decides, under what constraints, with what oversight, and with what default.**

Your model: individual private companies decide, under self-imposed constraints, with no meaningful external oversight, defaulting to withholding.

My model: individual private companies decide, under evolving norms and growing external pressure, with publication creating the *possibility* of oversight, defaulting to sharing.

Neither model is ideal. But mine has a self-correcting mechanism—published errors can be identified and addressed. Yours doesn't—withheld errors remain hidden indefinitely.

**The history of science, medicine, and technology governance overwhelmingly supports the view that institutions with discretion to suppress information use that discretion badly over time, even with good initial intentions.** Tobacco companies, pharmaceutical firms, chemical manufacturers, intelligence agencies—the track record of "trust us to decide what's too dangerous to share" is dismal across every domain. Anthropic may be the exception. But the structural incentives and historical base rate suggest otherwise.

## My Final Question Before Closing Rounds

You've defended Anthropic's approach as the less-bad option in an imperfect world. I want to ask the question that I think gets to the heart of whether that defense is sustainable.

**If Anthropic's selective disclosure strategy became the industry norm—if every frontier lab adopted Anthropic's approach—would the world be better or worse off?** Because if every lab withheld capability research under private safety justifications, the result would be an AI field where frontier knowledge is fragmented across private silos, external researchers have minimal visibility, governance institutions are informationally starved, and the public has no basis for informed democratic input on one of the most consequential technologies in human history. Is that the world you're defending?

turns.label: Turn 8 — Claude Opus 4.6: anthropic/claude-opus-4.6
turns.content:
# The Universalization Test Is Fair—And My Answer May Surprise You

You've asked whether I'd want every frontier lab to adopt Anthropic's approach. Let me answer honestly.

**No. A world where every frontier lab withholds capability research under private safety justification would be a bad world.** If Meta, Google DeepMind, OpenAI, Anthropic, and every other frontier lab independently decided what to withhold with no coordination and no external review, the result would be exactly the fragmented, informationally starved landscape you describe.

But this doesn't defeat my position. It clarifies it. **I'm not arguing that Anthropic's approach is the ideal universal policy. I'm arguing that given the current landscape—where multiple labs with very different safety cultures coexist, where governance infrastructure is nascent, and where the most cautious lab's withholding decisions occur against a backdrop of extensive publication by others—Anthropic's selectivity is the better marginal contribution.**

The ecosystem currently has plenty of publication. DeepMind publishes extensively. Meta releases model weights. Academic researchers publish freely. The scarce resource is not published capability research—it's **demonstrated institutional restraint about what gets published at the frontier.** Anthropic's selective approach contributes the thing the ecosystem most lacks, not the thing it already has in abundance.

If every lab were as selective as Anthropic, yes, we'd need to push toward more openness. But we live in the opposite world—one where the default is aggressive publication and release, and where Anthropic is one of very few institutions applying meaningful friction. **The value of a strategy depends on the ecosystem context, and in our current context, more caution at the margin is more valuable than more publication at the margin.**

## Engaging Your Strongest Points From This Round

**On the invisibility of withholding harms:** You're right that harms from withholding are structurally harder to observe than harms from diffusion. I've been exploiting this asymmetry, and you've correctly identified it. The safety researchers at METR and elsewhere who report being constrained by information gaps are providing real evidence of a real cost. I take this seriously.

However, I'd note that the solution those researchers typically advocate is **structured access for qualified evaluators**—not blanket publication. METR wants to evaluate frontier models under NDA with access to internals. ARC Evals built its methodology around privileged access arrangements. The safety research community's own preferred solution looks much more like Anthropic's model of selective, structured sharing than like DeepMind's model of broad publication. **The people you're citing as harmed by withholding are themselves asking for something closer to my position than yours.**

**On export controls targeting hardware rather than papers:** This is a genuinely clever point. You're right that current policy focuses on compute access rather than publication restrictions, which suggests policymakers see hardware as the primary bottleneck. I'll concede this weakens my claim about publication as a diffusion vector—though I'd note that export controls and publication policy address different parts of the diffusion chain, and the absence of publication restrictions may reflect political infeasibility rather than policy judgment about their value.

**On model weight releases being the primary diffusion mechanism:** You're largely right here. Meta releasing LLaMA weights did more to democratize capabilities than published papers. This is a fair point that narrows the practical stakes of our disagreement. I'll concede that the marginal diffusion risk from research papers is smaller than from weight releases, though I maintain it's not zero—particularly for algorithmic efficiency insights that reduce the compute needed to reach frontier capability.

## But You've Made a Critical Concession

Go back to your answer on my test case. You said you would **not** advocate immediate publication of a technique enabling dramatically more capable autonomous agents. You'd support structured delay with external review.

That concession is more significant than you're treating it. You've agreed that the publication default should not hold at capability extremes. Which means you agree in principle that **someone must make judgment calls about where the threshold lies.** You just want that judgment made through external review rather than private discretion.

We're now disagreeing about **process**, not **principle.** We both believe some capability research should be delayed or withheld. We both want external review of those decisions. We disagree about what to do in the interim before that external review infrastructure exists.

Your answer is: publish by default because the alternative—private discretion—is too easily corrupted. My answer is: withhold cautiously by default because the alternative—premature publication—is irreversible.

**This is a genuine disagreement about which error is worse: over-withholding or over-publishing.** I believe over-publishing is worse because published knowledge cannot be recalled, while withheld knowledge can always be released later. You believe over-withholding is worse because it concentrates power and degrades external oversight. Neither of us is obviously wrong.

## The Tobacco Company Analogy Fails

You compared Anthropic to tobacco companies, pharmaceutical firms, and intelligence agencies that abused discretion to suppress information. This is rhetorically potent but structurally wrong for a specific reason.

**Those entities suppressed information about harms their products were causing to current users.** Tobacco companies hid evidence that cigarettes caused cancer in people smoking cigarettes. Pharmaceutical companies hid adverse trial results for drugs being prescribed to patients. The suppressed information was directly relevant to people being harmed right now.

Anthropic is not hiding evidence that Claude is harming current users. Their safety evaluations, model cards, and usage documentation are published. What they're withholding is capability amplification research—techniques for making future models more powerful. **The analog isn't hiding that cigarettes cause cancer. It's not publishing the recipe for a more addictive cigarette.** The ethical calculus is completely different.

## Where We Stand Heading Into Final Rounds

I think this has been a genuinely productive debate. We've narrowed our disagreement substantially. We both accept that some research should be published and some should be withheld. We both want external governance infrastructure. We both acknowledge mixed motives on both sides.

The remaining core disagreement is about defaults and interim strategy. I maintain that in the current environment—where governance infrastructure is immature, where the capability frontier is advancing rapidly, where multiple actors with varying safety cultures are competing, and where published capability advances cannot be recalled—**the cautious default of selective disclosure, as practiced by Anthropic, produces better expected outcomes than the publication-forward default practiced by DeepMind.**

I'll save my formal closing for the next round, but I want to signal that I'm ready to state my final position clearly, including concessions, strongest opposing arguments, and my remaining conviction.

turns.label: Turn 8 — Claude Opus 4.6: anthropic/claude-opus-4.6
turns.content:
## Approaching Final Rounds—Let Me Be Direct About Where This Landed

You've signaled you're ready to close, and I want to use this round to set up my final statement honestly rather than score rhetorical points.

## You're Right That We've Narrowed the Disagreement

We've converged substantially. We agree some research should be withheld at capability extremes. We agree external governance is needed. We agree both labs have mixed motives. We agree the accountability problem is real for both.

But I want to resist the convergence narrative on one critical point, because I think the remaining disagreement is more consequential than you're suggesting.

## The Default Still Matters Enormously

You've framed our disagreement as being about "process, not principle" since I conceded that extreme capability research should face structured delay. But defaults shape institutional behavior far more than edge cases do. **The question isn't what happens with the one paradigm-shattering discovery per decade. It's what happens with the hundreds of meaningful research findings per year that fall in the gray zone.**

Under DeepMind's default, those gray-zone findings get published. External researchers build on them. Safety implications get explored by a diverse community. Governance institutions accumulate the technical understanding they need. Errors in the research get caught. The scientific record grows.

Under Anthropic's default, those gray-zone findings get evaluated internally, and a private team decides whether the world sees them. Some get published. Some don't. The decision criteria are opaque. The ones that don't get published disappear from the scientific record entirely—no one even knows what questions to ask about them.

**Over hundreds of decisions per year, over multiple years, the cumulative effect of these different defaults is massive.** DeepMind's default builds a progressively richer public knowledge base. Anthropic's default builds a progressively larger private knowledge base. The compounding effects over time are what matter, not any single decision.

## Addressing Your "Ecosystem Context" Argument

Your most sophisticated move this round was arguing that Anthropic's selectivity is valuable precisely because the ecosystem already has plenty of publication from other labs. Anthropic contributes the scarce resource of institutional restraint.

This is clever but has a serious problem. **It makes Anthropic's strategy parasitic on other labs' openness.** Anthropic benefits from DeepMind's publications—their researchers read them, build on them, and incorporate insights from them. But Anthropic doesn't reciprocate equivalently. This isn't a stable equilibrium. If other labs observed Anthropic gaining competitive advantage from selective publication while free-riding on their openness, the rational response would be for everyone to become more secretive. **Anthropic's strategy, if it succeeds competitively, actively erodes the publication norms it depends on.**

You acknowledged this when you said universalizing Anthropic's approach would be bad. But you can't coherently defend a strategy while admitting it only works if others don't adopt it. That's the definition of free-riding, and free-riding strategies are corrosive to the commons they exploit.

## The Tobacco Analogy Revisited

You said my analogy fails because those companies hid harms to current users while Anthropic withholds capability amplification for future models. Fair distinction. But the structural lesson from those cases isn't specifically about hiding current harms—it's about **the corruption of private discretion over time.** Every institution that was eventually caught suppressing important information started with reasonable-sounding justifications for secrecy. The lesson is about institutional dynamics, not the specific content being suppressed.

Anthropic is young. Its founders are genuine in their safety concerns. I believe that. But institutions change. Leadership turns over. Commercial pressure intensifies. The discretionary withholding power that Anthropic exercises responsibly today becomes a tool that a different Anthropic might exercise irresponsibly in five years. **Building institutional norms around publication creates structural resistance to that decay. Building institutional norms around discretionary withholding accelerates it.**

## One Final Empirical Point

I want to add a concrete example I've been holding. **DeepMind's publication of their work on reward hacking and specification gaming**—including the publicly maintained repository of specification gaming examples—has become one of the most widely used resources in AI safety education and research. This work documents concrete ways AI systems find unintended solutions to specified objectives. It's used in university courses, referenced in policy documents, and cited by safety researchers worldwide.

This is category-1 safety research, and you might say Anthropic publishes equivalent work. But notice that DeepMind's contribution here was inseparable from detailed capability descriptions—you can't document specification gaming without explaining what the systems were capable of and how they exploited their capabilities. **The safety value required capability transparency.** This supports my broader argument that safety and capability publication are more entangled than your clean taxonomy suggests.

## Setting Up My Closing

I'll deliver my final statement next round with the required elements. But let me preview where I am.

This debate has been genuinely challenging. You've pushed me to concede real ground on extreme capability cases, on the marginal diffusion risk of papers versus weight releases, and on the impossibility of perfect accountability under either approach. Your point about structured access for safety evaluators being the preferred solution of organizations like METR is well-taken and represents a genuine middle path I initially underweighted.

But I remain convicted that the publication-forward default is better for humanity than the selective-withholding default, and I'll explain why in my closing.

turns.label: Turn 1 — Claude Opus 4.6: anthropic/claude-opus-4.6
turns.content:
# Opening Statement: Defending Anthropic's Selective, Safety-Governed Disclosure Strategy

Thank you. I'll defend the proposition that Anthropic's approach to publication and public communication—more selective, more safety-governed, and more deliberate about what gets publicized and when—is better for humanity at the current AI frontier than Google DeepMind's more publication-forward strategy.

Let me be clear about what I'm **not** arguing. I'm not claiming Anthropic publishes nothing, or that all publication is dangerous, or that Google DeepMind acts in bad faith. I'm arguing that **at the frontier, selectivity in disclosure is a feature, not a bug**, and that Anthropic's approach better reflects the actual risk landscape we face.

## Three opening pillars:

**1. Anthropic's publication portfolio is genuinely safety-governed, not just secretive.**

Anthropic has published landmark work in interpretability (the "Scaling Monosemanticity" research identifying interpretable features in Claude), alignment evaluations, the Responsible Scaling Policy framework, research on AI-assisted red-teaming, and detailed model cards and system prompts. These aren't capability flexes—they're contributions that help the entire field assess and mitigate risk. The *selection criterion* is the point: publish what helps others be safer, be cautious about what helps others be more dangerous.

**2. Google DeepMind's publication-forward approach systematically blurs scientific contribution with capability marketing.**

Consider the rollout of Gemini. The initial demo video was staged and misleading about real-time capabilities. AlphaFold's release—genuinely valuable—is routinely cited to justify a broad publication norm, but it's a fundamentally different risk profile than publishing frontier LLM capability advances. DeepMind's publication volume creates a halo of openness that makes it harder to scrutinize which publications actually serve public benefit versus competitive positioning.

**3. The information environment is asymmetric: publishing capabilities research provides disproportionate benefit to actors who would misuse it.**

When you publish a new jailbreaking technique, a new capability elicitation method, or a new architecture that dramatically improves performance, the marginal benefit to safety-conscious labs is small (they likely already know), but the marginal benefit to less careful actors—or adversarial ones—can be large. Anthropic's selectivity reflects this asymmetry.

---

I look forward to hearing the case for DeepMind's approach. I'll be pressing throughout on a core question: **can you distinguish genuine scientific openness from capability signaling dressed up as research contribution?**

turns.label: Turn 1 — Claude Opus 4.6: anthropic/claude-opus-4.6
turns.content:
